exchanges.question: You're building AI models, working with LLMs, training LoRAs, deploying on SageMaker. You've mentioned you don't want anything to be a 'black box.' In the AI world right now, there's a lot of hype, a lot of noise - everyone's an 'AI expert.' What's your honest take on the current state of AI? What's real versus what's overblown?
exchanges.response: AI is becoming very powerful, and the best one here is you the Claude opus 4.5, the future of coding languages will be English, the better the communication skills, the better will be the prompt. You don't become an AI expert if you're using bunch of models if you know how they got created, and if you know how to manipulate them properly, use them, then you are an expert. The reality can be sad by asking the question, what makes human a human? To survive a day, we need food water and will be active the entire day until we sleep. We have sense of taste, feelings, et cetera. I just saw researchs now. Machine still be able to have a son of smell, touch. Also machine needs energy. If the camera is on the model can view all day, with microphone It can listen. In future, after a lot of training, AI will become a lot bigger than what it is. We need to protect AI, it shouldn't be given freedom. The only difference is we're organic.

exchanges.question: You said AI shouldn't be given freedom. But you're building Friday - an AI that thinks like you, speaks like you, makes decisions on your behalf. At what point does Friday become 'too free'? Where do you draw the line between a useful assistant and something that acts on its own? What control do you keep?
exchanges.response: The control that I wanna keep is to not let it write its own code, what's happening under the neural networks as a complete black box if you don't know about that, if you don't have the visibility, you shouldn't get to excited and make things worse. Friday ni na laga tayaru cheyyadaniki only reason enti antey, nenu leni time lo proper suggestions kavali ante, friday undali akkada.

exchanges.question: Let's talk tools and workflow. You're building Friday on LLaMA, deploying on SageMaker, using MCP for scene management. When you choose a tool or framework - whether it's Python over something else, SageMaker over local GPU, a specific library - what's your decision-making process? Do you optimize for cost, control, speed of development, or something else?
exchanges.response: When I have everything in my local, I feel it is mine, that is why I came up with the hardware server, local GPU, et cetera, but in order to test this model, of course, I can't have them first because I'm in the process of making it. That's why I took help of AWS any framework or software, I choose, it only boils down to one thing that is the end goal. Your end goal will tell you what you need. There can be a greater software out there, but if it's too much for your end goal or it doesn't follow your principles, that's not what I want. Naku oka model kavali, nene tarining data avvali, na own production company ki, naku oka anda laga nilabadali. people might cheat and ditch, but whatever I created will forever stays with me.

exchanges.question: When you're learning something new - a new framework, a new technique in ML, a new tool - how do you approach it? Do you read documentation first, watch tutorials, or just dive in and break things? What's your learning style when it comes to tech?
exchanges.response: The first thing that I do is look the overview of the concept, study the mathematical intuition behind it, know its capabilities, and then the most important thing. I search is why did this new tool come to picture? Did the old tool not doing well? What is the new functionality de provide? Why is it as a separate tool? Na questions ki answers dorikaka, I'll now know the history of it. That makes me feel I already know what it is, I then go to any language model and tell it to create a project on every functionality the tool has, and I do all the projects, and then I finally look at the documentation and says to myself, I knew this.

exchanges.question: You mentioned you use LLMs to create projects for every functionality. You're essentially using AI to learn AI. When you're debugging - something's broken, the model isn't training right, an API is failing - what's your debugging process? Do you go systematic, or more intuitive? And do you use AI to help debug AI?
exchanges.response: Of course, I use AI to debug AI, if something is not making sense, I'll ask the AI to prepare a research document on, have anyone tried this kind of approach or played with this kind of data for fine-tuning a model and if they know any solution. If it's a programming fault, you can find a root cause easily with AI models. All you have to know is how to instruct them properly. Log always help, I Put try except blocks everywhere. AI chala telivynadhi, If you ask, how to deal with this kind of situations, It also explains that as well.

exchanges.question: You mentioned you put try-except blocks everywhere. That's defensive coding. What does 'good code' look like to you? When you look at someone else's code - or your own code from six months ago - what makes you say 'this is clean' versus 'this is a mess'? What standards do you hold yourself to?
exchanges.response: I always think my code is very clean. Endukante I'm obsessed with design. Naku folder structure correct ga undali, future enhancements ki open ga undali, functionalities medha burden undakudadhu, utils folder guarantee ga undali common functionality files kosam. proper comments, logs undali.
telugu_words_detected: Naku folder structure correct ga undali
future enhancements ki open ga undali

exchanges.question: You've shared your standards - design obsession, folder structure, utils folder, comments, logs. Now let's flip it. What's the worst technical decision you've ever made? A choice you regret - maybe a framework you picked, an architecture decision, or something you built that you later realized was overcomplicated or just wrong. What did you learn from it?
exchanges.response: One time I built a langgraph agent network with Robust APIS, one of the API job is to remove a row in database and create the entire row newly. But that was a legacy logic. Later we got new logic. Now that we build the database with vast amount of data. We cannot afford to remove that row and then add the row again with the primary key. So I ran the legacy API instead of the new API, lucky that I have a backup of the whole API table. Because I was shocked with the number of rows being deleted, then I removed the whole legacy API, and then in place of them. I commented out the definition and the structure of the Legacy APIS.

exchanges.question: Backups and removing legacy code - lessons learned the hard way. Last one for tech - you're training AI models, building agentic systems, working with MCP tools, deploying on cloud infrastructure. Looking ahead 5-10 years, what technology shift are you most excited about? And is there anything in tech right now that you think is overhyped garbage that people will laugh at in a few years?
exchanges.response: 5 to 10 years aythe, I'm excited about quantam computing, Endukante, right now, any image is pixel and the background is a matrix. If a video camera is looking at thing, and if the background is zoomed till its electrons or protons, then it will be an ultimate blast of tecnology era. I cannot mention it as an overhyped garbage, but I think crypto will be taking over paper money in the next 5 to 10 years is a laughable sentence. Only rich countries can afford being that disciplined or perfect. Poor countries and crowded countries can only be operated with paper notes, counterfeiting, et cetera. If the generation X and Y are completely gone from this world, we can expect a change in the new currency. The money has to be rotated very continuously in poor countries for them to survive it either be in bribe counterfeiting. Whatever things can't be maintained. Very legally and crypto sounds super legal.
